stimulus:
[Table: Mean Time (in Seconds) Spent per Inflorescence for Four Pollinator Genera Visiting Short-Style and Long-Style Primula Flowers. Columns: pollinator genus, seconds per intact short-style, seconds per damaged short-style, seconds per intact long-style, seconds per damaged long-style. Rows: Bombus—3.2, 5.1, 3.9, 6.0; Anthidium—2.4, 3.7, 3.5, 5.2; Eristalis—2.8, 2.6, 3.0, 4.8; Vanessa—2.1, 1.9, 3.4, 4.6.]

Researchers Sofia Lindqvist and Tomás Reyes proposed that physical damage to Primula inflorescences (e.g., petal tears) alters how long pollinators linger at a flower, but in a way that depends jointly on the pollinator’s genus and on the flower’s style morph (short-style vs. long-style). In particular, they concluded from a 2024 study that floral damage increased the mean time spent per flower across pollinator genera for long-style Primula, but increased the mean time per flower in only some pollinator genera for short-style Primula.
stem: Which choice best describes data from the table that support the researchers’ conclusion?

answerOptions.content: Pollinators belonging to the genus Eristalis spent 2.8 seconds on intact short-style flowers and 3.0 seconds on intact long-style flowers.
Compared with pollinators belonging to the genus Anthidium, pollinators belonging to the genus Vanessa spent less time on damaged short-style flowers.
For long-style flowers, damage led to longer times per flower in all four pollinator genera, whereas for short-style flowers, damage led to longer times per flower only in Bombus and Anthidium.
Damage led to shorter times per long-style flower in three pollinator genera (Bombus, Anthidium, and Eristalis), whereas it led to longer times per long-style flower in Vanessa.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it accurately characterizes the table data and matches both halves of the researchers’ conclusion. For long-style flowers, mean time increased with damage in all four genera (3.9→6.0, 3.5→5.2, 3.0→4.8, 3.4→4.6); for short-style flowers, time increased with damage only in Bombus (3.2→5.1) and Anthidium (2.4→3.7), while Eristalis (2.8→2.6) and Vanessa (2.1→1.9) showed slight decreases.

Choice A is incorrect because it merely reports two cell values and characterizes neither the role of damage nor the across-genus pattern. Choice B is incorrect because it compares two genera on a single condition and so does not bear on the joint genus-and-morph pattern in the conclusion. Choice D is incorrect because it misreads the table: damage did not lead to shorter times per long-style flower in those genera; long-style values rose under damage in all four genera.
